disclaimer: These are market benchmarks based on observed India VC deals in 2023-2025. Terms vary significantly by investor, sector, and negotiation. Always consult a CA and startup lawyer before signing any term sheet. This is educational information, not legal advice.

pre_seed.label: Pre-Seed
pre_seed.typical_stage: Idea to MVP — 0 to 12 months post-incorporation

valuation_post_money.note: Valuation is largely a negotiated number at pre-seed — traction, team pedigree, and market matter more than any formula

dilution.note: 15% is a fair pre-seed dilution. Above 20% is founder-unfavorable unless the investor brings exceptional value

instrument.india_note: CCDs are most common for Indian entities due to RBI regulations on FDI. SAFEs are increasingly used by founders with US investor exposure. Avoid straight equity at pre-seed — it creates complex cap table issues early.
instrument.avoid: Equity shares — too early for cap table complexity

board.observer: Optional — 1 observer right for lead investor writing > ₹50L
board.note: No board seats is standard at pre-seed. An observer right (attend but not vote) is acceptable for a large angel.

esop_pool.note: Investors typically want a 10% ESOP pool created pre-money. This dilutes founders, not investors. 10% is standard and fair.

anti_dilution.note: Anti-dilution protection is rare and inappropriate at pre-seed. If an investor insists on it at this stage, treat it as a yellow flag.

pro_rata.note: Pro-rata rights are uncommon at pre-seed. Occasionally offered to large angels (writing ₹1Cr+) as a 'participation right' — acceptable if it's for the next round only.

liquidation_preference.note: 1x non-participating is acceptable at pre-seed. Participating liquidation preference (double-dipping) is a red flag. Any preference above 1x at this stage is investor-favorable to an unreasonable degree.

information_rights: Annual audited accounts within 6 months of year end
pre_seed.lock_in: 12-18 months for founders (vesting schedule if not already agreed)
pre_seed.due_diligence_timeline: 2-4 weeks (lightweight — term sheet review + basic KYC)
typical_docs: SSA (Share Subscription Agreement) or CCD Agreement
founder_tips: Get the DPIIT Recognition certificate before raising — many angels won't write cheques without it and it unlocks Section 56(2)(viib) exemption from angel tax.
Fix co-founder equity and vesting BEFORE the first external round. A handshake deal that falls apart post-funding is a startup killer.
Raise the minimum viable amount at pre-seed — enough for 12-18 months runway. Over-raising at pre-seed creates valuation expectations that can trap you at seed.
Negotiate a post-money SAFE cap that's 2-3x your current pre-seed valuation. This gives investors downside protection without capping your upside too aggressively.
Always get a 'most favoured nation' (MFN) clause if you're doing rolling SAFEs — it ensures earlier investors get the same terms as later ones in the same round.
red_flags: Participating liquidation preference at pre-seed — this lets investors 'double dip' on exit (get their money back AND share in upside). Walk away.
Full-ratchet anti-dilution — if your valuation ever drops, investors get new shares at your expense. Only BBWA (broad-based weighted average) is acceptable.
Veto rights on hiring/firing decisions — operational control should stay with founders at this stage.
Drag-along rights at pre-seed in isolation without a threshold — can force a sale you don't agree with. Ensure minimum drag threshold is the majority of the cap table.

seed.label: Seed
seed.typical_stage: MVP to early revenue — 10 to 500 paying customers or users
typical_investors: Institutional seed funds (Blume Ventures, Stellaris, 3one4 Capital, Kae Capital, ah! Ventures)

valuation_post_money.note: Seed valuations in India have compressed in 2023-25. ₹30-40 Cr post-money for a capital-efficient team with real traction is a fair range.

dilution.note: 18-20% is market standard at seed. Anything above 25% will make Series A pricing very difficult for founders.

instrument.alternative: CCPS (Compulsorily Convertible Preference Shares) for larger seed rounds
instrument.india_note: CCDs remain dominant at seed stage with Indian institutional VCs. US-style preferred equity (CCPS) is increasingly used for rounds above ₹10 Cr. The distinction matters for RBI FEMA compliance if you have foreign investors.

board.composition: 2 founders + 1 investor director (5-seat board possible with independent)
board.note: Standard seed board is 2F + 1I. An independent director can be added at this stage — it's good governance and makes Series A negotiations easier.

esop_pool.note: 10% ESOP pool created pre-money is standard. Investors push for 15% — push back to 10-12%. The pool gets refreshed at Series A anyway.

anti_dilution.note: BBWA is the market standard and is fair to both sides. Full-ratchet anti-dilution is unacceptable at any stage. BBWA only triggers in a 'down round' — protect yourself by building in exceptions for ESOP grants and pre-agreed convertibles.

pro_rata.note: Pro-rata rights for the lead investor are standard at seed. It's reasonable for angels writing ₹1Cr+ to also ask for pro-rata. Uncapped pro-rata obligations can create problems at Series A if all seed investors want to maintain their percentage.

liquidation_preference.note: Standard and founder-friendly. 1x non-participating means investors get their money back OR convert to equity — they can't do both. Push back hard on participating preference; 1x participating is sometimes accepted but avoid it if possible.

seed.drag_along: Yes — standard. Protects investors in M&A. Ensure threshold is majority of all shareholders (not just preference shareholders).
seed.right_of_first_refusal: Yes — investors get first right to buy shares if a founder wants to sell.
seed.tag_along: Yes — investors can participate in any secondary sale on same terms.
information_rights: Monthly MIS (management information system) — P&L, balance sheet, cash position
Annual audited financial statements within 6 months of year end
Cap table updates within 30 days of any new issuances
Material event notifications (C-suite change, major contract, litigation)
typical_docs: SHA (full form — covers board, information rights, transfer restrictions)
IP Assignment Agreements for founders and early employees
founder_tips: At seed, fight hardest on post-money valuation cap rather than dilution percentage — a higher cap means less dilution at the same round size.
Negotiate a 'pay-to-play' clause: investors who don't participate pro-rata in future rounds lose their anti-dilution protection. This protects you from investors who take rights but don't follow on.
Try to keep your ESOP pool below 12% pre-seed. The standard investor ask is 15% — the difference significantly affects founder dilution.

Always negotiate a 'sunset clause' on ROFR (Right of First Refusal) — ideally it expires 3 years post-close or at Series A, whichever is earlier. Permanent ROFR creates secondary market complications.
Get a legal opinion on your CCD terms before signing — specifically ensure conversion ratios are clearly defined and don't have hidden adverse adjustment clauses.
red_flags: Full participating liquidation preference — the investor both gets their money back AND shares in the equity upside. It's economically equivalent to double-dipping and is genuinely harmful to founders on exit.
Cumulative dividends on preference shares — these compound over time and can make an acquisition economics look worse than they are. Non-cumulative is the standard.
Investor consent required for every new hire above a certain level — operational drag that slows you down. Reserve consent rights for C-suite and material contracts only.
Automatic conversion at IPO or acquisition blocked by investor veto — ensure automatic conversion provisions cannot be individually blocked by minority investors.

series_a.label: Series A
series_a.typical_stage: Proven PMF, scaling revenue — ₹3-25 Cr ARR or $500K-$3M ARR

valuation_post_money.note: In India 2024-25, B2B SaaS typically prices at 5-8x ARR multiple at Series A. Consumer/D2C at 3-5x. Revenue-based multiples are now common — show 18 months of financial history.

dilution.note: 22-25% is typical for a $5M round. If you're raising from a top-tier VC, they'll push for 25-30% — negotiate hard if you have competing term sheets.

instrument.note: Series A is a full equity round in India. CCPS is standard for institutional VC investment. It comes with liquidation preference, anti-dilution, and board rights. The CCPS converts to ordinary equity at IPO or liquidity event.

board.composition: 2 founders + 1 lead VC director + 1 independent director + 1 optional (for large co-investor)
board.note: Independent director is legally required for private companies with paid-up share capital > ₹10 Cr or turnover > ₹100 Cr. Get a credible independent director from your network — it builds investor confidence.

esop_pool.note: Series A investors will typically want the ESOP pool refreshed to 10-15% post-round. This dilutes existing shareholders. Budget this into your valuation negotiations.

anti_dilution.note: Same standard as seed. Make sure BBWA calculations are specified in the SHA — ambiguity in formula is a common source of disputes at later rounds.

pro_rata.threshold: All Series A investors typically get pro-rata in Series B
pro_rata.note: Pro-rata is critical for VCs to maintain ownership in later rounds. Budget for this in future round models — if all Series A investors exercise pro-rata, it limits the new capital you can raise from new investors.

liquidation_preference.note: This is the industry standard at Series A. Some VCs will push for 1x participating — resist it. The difference: on a $30M acquisition, 1x non-participating gives investors $5M (their money) then founders split the rest. 1x participating gives investors $5M PLUS their pro-rata equity share of remaining $25M. Always non-participating.

series_a.drag_along: Yes — standard. Ensure threshold is 75-80% of all shareholders by value (not just preference shareholders) to prevent a minority blocking a majority-approved sale.
series_a.tag_along: Yes — standard. On any secondary sale, all investors get the right to sell on same terms.
series_a.founder_vesting: Re-vesting of remaining founder shares over 2 years with 6-month cliff is common. Negotiate to exclude shares already vested from prior arrangements.
information_rights: Monthly P&L, balance sheet, and cash flow statement within 15 days of month end
Quarterly board meetings (mandatory by law for companies with independent directors)
Annual budget approved by board before fiscal year start
Annual statutory audit completed within 6 months of year end
Prior board approval for major decisions: acquisition, large capex, key management hiring, new business lines
series_a.due_diligence_timeline: 4-8 weeks (full legal, financial, technical, and commercial diligence)
founder_tips: At Series A, try to get competing term sheets — even a soft indication from a second VC meaningfully improves your leverage on valuation and terms.
Negotiate 'carve-outs' from the drag-along right: employee ESOP exercises, small secondary sales to known buyers, and transfers to founder family trusts should not trigger drag-along.

Push for a 'most favoured nation' provision that applies going forward — if you offer better terms to any future investor at Series A, existing investors get the same. This prevents dilutive future tranches from being structured against you.

Get clarity on 'reserved matters' (decisions requiring investor board approval) upfront. A long list of reserved matters is an operational tax. Push for a shorter list focused on material transactions (>$1M), M&A, and capital structure changes.
Understand the 'pay-to-play' structure at Series A: investors who don't follow pro-rata at Series B often convert to ordinary equity. This incentivizes your cap table to stay supportive.
red_flags: Participating liquidation preference of 1x or more — on any exit less than 5x, founders are materially worse off. Run the math: on a $30M exit with $5M raised, 1x participating LP means investors take $5M + their equity share. Non-participating means investors choose the higher of $5M OR their equity share — much better for founders.
Veto rights on M&A for minority shareholders — a 15% VC blocking a 75% shareholder-approved acquisition is not market standard. Drag-along should require 75%+ majority, not unanimity.
Option to invest in future rounds at the current round price — gives investors a 'call option' on your company at below-market rates. Each future fundraise should price fresh.

Broad 'material adverse change' clauses that let investors exit post-signing — insist on objective material adverse change definitions that are limited to fundamental changes in the business (bankruptcy, fraud, loss of key license) not market conditions.

glossary.CCD: Compulsorily Convertible Debenture — a debt instrument that must convert into equity shares at a future date or event. Preferred by Indian VCs for FDI compliance. Accrues interest (typically 0.001%) until conversion.
glossary.CCPS: Compulsorily Convertible Preference Shares — a type of preference share that must convert to equity at IPO or acquisition. Standard instrument for formal VC rounds at Seed and Series A in India.
glossary.SAFE: Simple Agreement for Future Equity — a US-originated convertible instrument (created by Y Combinator) that converts to equity at the next priced round. The 'India SAFE' or iSAFE (used by 100X.VC) is adapted for Indian law.
glossary.BBWA: Broad-Based Weighted Average — the standard anti-dilution formula in India. In a down round, it adjusts the investor's conversion price based on the weighted average of old and new round prices, diluting impact across all parties. Better for founders than full-ratchet.
glossary.Pro-rata: The right of an existing investor to maintain their ownership percentage by investing in future rounds. If you own 15% today and a new round raises $10M, pro-rata lets you invest enough to remain at 15% post-round.
glossary.Liquidation Preference: Priority claim on proceeds in a sale or liquidation event. 1x non-participating = investors get their money back first, then everyone splits the rest. 1x participating = investors get their money back first AND share proportionally in the rest (double dip).
glossary.Drag-along: Investor right to force all shareholders to agree to a sale if a specified majority (usually 75%+) approves. Prevents a minority shareholder from blocking a majority-approved exit.
glossary.Tag-along: Right of shareholders to join ('tag along') in any sale of shares by another shareholder on the same terms. Protects minority investors from being left behind in a secondary transaction.
glossary.ROFR: Right of First Refusal — if a founder wants to sell shares, existing investors have the right to buy those shares at the same price before anyone else. Common in all term sheets.
glossary.ESOP: Employee Stock Option Plan — a pool of shares reserved for granting to employees, advisors, and future hires. Options vest over time (typically 4 years with 1-year cliff). The ESOP pool is created pre-money, diluting existing shareholders.
glossary.No-shop: A clause preventing founders from soliciting competing term sheets for a fixed period after signing a term sheet. Typically 30-45 days. After this period, the term sheet lapses or is renegotiated.
glossary.Representations & Warranties: Legally binding statements made by the founders confirming the accuracy of information provided during due diligence (no undisclosed liabilities, IP is clean, no pending litigation, etc.). Breach of R&W can result in founder liability.

negotiation_tips: Always get at least 2 term sheets before signing — even a soft indication from a second investor creates leverage. Single-term-sheet situations result in worse founder outcomes 80% of the time.

Separate 'economic terms' (valuation, dilution, liquidation preference) from 'governance terms' (board rights, reserved matters, information rights) when negotiating. Investors often concede governance in exchange for better economics — know which you care about more.

Use a startup-experienced lawyer from day one. Non-specialist lawyers may not flag uncommon Indian VC provisions like 'step-in rights' or 'conversion ratio adjustments.' Top startup law firms in India: Trilegal, Khaitan & Co, Cyril Amarchand Mangaldas (CAM), Shardul Amarchand Mangaldas (SAM).
On the ESOP pool: investors typically want 15% pre-money. Counter-offer 10% and land at 12%. Every 1% of ESOP pool reduces founder ownership — on a ₹100 Cr exit this matters significantly.

On valuation: focus on post-money valuation, not pre-money. Many Indian term sheets state pre-money valuation which is easier to manipulate. Fix the post-money number in the term sheet to prevent surprises.

Read the SHA drag-along clause carefully — specifically, what constitutes the 'required majority' to approve a drag. If the required majority is defined as a majority of preference shareholders only (not all shareholders), a VC with 25% ownership could theoretically drag you into a sale you don't want.
